Musical genre

**Afro rock** is a [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") [music genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that fuses Western rock instrumentation with traditional African musical elements, which emerged in the late 1960s and early 1970s.

## Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Afro_rock&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Influences")]

Afro rock draws heavily from traditional African music styles and genres, including [Afrobeat](/wiki/Afrobeat "Afrobeat"), [Highlife](/wiki/Highlife "Highlife"), [Jùjú](/wiki/J%C3%B9j%C3%BA_music "Jùjú music"), and [Soukous](/wiki/Soukous "Soukous"). It is also influenced by Western musical styles such as [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), and [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"). Prominent Western artists and bands like [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin"), [Santana](/wiki/Santana_(band) "Santana (band)"), [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix"), and La Fayette contributed to the development of Afro rock through their avant-garde approaches to rock music. In the late 1960s, bands including the Super Eagles and Psychedelic Aliens made important developments to the genre.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[Fela Kuti](/wiki/Fela_Kuti "Fela Kuti"), a [Nigerian](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") musician and [activist](/wiki/Activism "Activism"), played an important role in the genesis of Afro rock through his influence on the development of [Afrobeat](/wiki/Afrobeat "Afrobeat"), a genre incorporating elements from both traditional African music with [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The lyrical content of the genre commonly addresses themes of social justice, economic inequality, freedom and cultural identity, relevant to the [political](/wiki/Political_movement "Political movement") and [social movements](/wiki/Social_movement "Social movement") in [post-colonial](/wiki/Postcolonialism "Postcolonialism") African societies during the mid-20th century.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Lyrics are typically delivered in a combination of local [African languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Africa "Languages of Africa") and [Pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") English or French.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Afro_rock&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

The instrumentation in Afro rock typically includes [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), [bass guitars](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar"), [drum kits](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit"), [keyboards](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard"), and [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), alongside traditional African instruments such as the [talking drum](/wiki/Talking_drum "Talking drum"), [mbira](/wiki/Mbira "Mbira"), [balafon](/wiki/Balafon "Balafon"), and [kora](/wiki/Kora_(instrument) "Kora (instrument)").[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) The electric guitar often functions as the lead instrument. Guitarists commonly employ techniques such as [riffing](/wiki/Riffing "Riffing"), [improvisation](/wiki/Musical_improvisation "Musical improvisation"), and the use of effects, particularly [wah-wah](/wiki/Wah-wah_pedal "Wah-wah pedal") and [fuzz](/wiki/Fuzz_pedal "Fuzz pedal") pedals, to modify tonal characteristics and create a distinctive sound profile.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Collectie_Nationaal_Museum_van_Wereldculturen_TM-6335-1a_Enkelvellige_tonvormige_buistrom%2C_onderdeel_van_een_set_Cuba.jpg/250px-Collectie_Nationaal_Museum_van_Wereldculturen_TM-6335-1a_Enkelvellige_tonvormige_buistrom%2C_onderdeel_van_een_set_Cuba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Collectie_Nationaal_Museum_van_Wereldculturen_TM-6335-1a_Enkelvellige_tonvormige_buistrom,_onderdeel_van_een_set_Cuba.jpg)

A conga drum

[Percussion](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") is also an essential component in Afro rock which combines standard Western [drum kits](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit") with a variety of African and Afro-Caribbean percussion instruments, including [congas](/wiki/Conga "Conga"), [djembes](/wiki/Djembe "Djembe"), and [shekeres](/wiki/Shekere "Shekere").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Rhythmically, the genre frequently uses complex devices including [polyrhythms](/wiki/Polyrhythm "Polyrhythm"), [syncopation](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation"), and [irregular time signatures](/wiki/Irregular_time_signatures "Irregular time signatures"), taking from contemporary African genres such as [Highlife](/wiki/Highlife "Highlife"), [Soukous](/wiki/Soukous "Soukous") and [Jùjú](/wiki/J%C3%B9j%C3%BA_music "Jùjú music").[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Horn](/wiki/Horn_(instrument) "Horn (instrument)") sections, often comprising [saxophones](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"), [trumpets](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), and [trombones](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone"), are also used in some Afro rock ensembles.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Performance](/wiki/Performance "Performance") in the genre is often defined by features such as extended [instrumental passages](/wiki/Instrumental "Instrumental") and interactions between performing musicians. Live performances may include [jam sessions](/wiki/Jam_session "Jam session") and spontaneously rearranged [compositions](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") different from [studio recordings](/wiki/Studio_recording "Studio recording"). Vocal delivery ranges from melodic singing to [call-and-response](/wiki/Call_and_response "Call and response"), inspired from both Western and African performance styles.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Notable Afro rock musicians in Nigeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Afro_rock&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable Afro rock musicians in Nigeria")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Afro_rock "Special:EditPage/Afro rock") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Afro rock"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Afro+rock%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Afro+rock%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Afro+rock%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Afro+rock%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Afro+rock%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Afro+rock%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Afro rock emerged in Nigeria during the late 1960s and early 1970s, during periods of political unrest in the duration and the aftermath of the [Nigerian Civil War](/wiki/Nigerian_Civil_War "Nigerian Civil War").

[The Hykkers](/w/index.php?title=The_Hykkers&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Hykkers (page does not exist)"), formed in the early 1960s by Nigerian students, initially performed covers of British rock songs. By the late 1960s, they began integrating Nigerian rhythms and melodies with Western instrumentation, which contributed to the development of Afro rock. Their music gained prominence with the support of [Fela Kuti](/wiki/Fela_Kuti "Fela Kuti"), then a well-known figure in the [Nigerian music scene](/wiki/Nigerian_music "Nigerian music"). During the Nigerian Civil War, the band was reportedly captured by the Nigerian army, and their music was repurposed to boost morale and celebrate military victories. Notable [tracks](/wiki/Music_track "Music track") by The Hykkers include *I Wanna Break Thru*.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Formed in the early 1970s, [Wrinkar Experience](/w/index.php?title=Wrinkar_Experience&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wrinkar Experience (page does not exist)") was a Nigerian Afro rock band that gained popularity with their single *Fuel for Lov*e, which became one of the highest-selling Nigerian singles of its time. The band, comprising members from [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon"), and [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), was known for their lively performances and mix of Afrobeat rhythms with rock elements. After extensive touring across [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), the group disbanded in 1977. Wrinklar Experience also produced the track *Ballad of a Sad Young Woman.*

[Ofege](/w/index.php?title=Ofege_band&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ofege band (page does not exist)") was a band formed by high school students in the early 1970s. Drawing inspiration from guitarists such as [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix") and [Carlos Santana](/wiki/Carlos_Santana "Carlos Santana"), Ofege used Afrobeat rhythms with elements of [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"), [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"). Their debut album, *Try and Love* (1973),[[20]](#cite_note-20) received critical acclaim for its creative approach and musical maturity, despite the young age of its members. Subsequent albums like *The Last of the Origins* (1976),[[21]](#cite_note-21) *Higher Plane Breeze* (1977) and *How Do You Feel* (1978)[[22]](#cite_note-22) also influenced the development of Afro rock.

Taiwo and Kehinde Lijadu, known as the [Lijadu Sisters](/wiki/Lijadu_Sisters "Lijadu Sisters"), were [twin sisters](/wiki/Twin "Twin") who gained recognition in the late 1960s and 1970s for their music in the style of [Afrobeat](/wiki/Afrobeat "Afrobeat"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") and [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Influenced by artists such as [Miriam Makeba](/wiki/Miriam_Makeba "Miriam Makeba"), [Aretha Franklin](/wiki/Aretha_Franklin "Aretha Franklin"), and their cousin [Fela Kuti](/wiki/Fela_Kuti "Fela Kuti"), the duo were known for their use of [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") and traditional rhythmic patterns in their music. Their albums, including *Danger* (1976), *Horizon Unlimited* (1979) and *Mother Africa* (1977), often carried political messages and addressed social issues of the time. The duo toured [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), collaborating with well-known musicians such as [Ginger Baker](/wiki/Ginger_Baker "Ginger Baker").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Formed in 1970 by guitarist and [organist](/wiki/Organist "Organist") Bob Miga, The Strangers were an Afro rock band that emerged in the period following the [Nigerian Civil War](/wiki/Nigerian_Civil_War "Nigerian Civil War"). They released three [singles](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") and a full album before disbanding. Their music featured funk-rock tunes using [fuzz](/wiki/Fuzz_pedal "Fuzz pedal") guitar and organ [riffs](/wiki/Riff "Riff").[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Osayomore Joseph](/wiki/Osayomore_Joseph "Osayomore Joseph") was a Nigerian musician and [political activist](/wiki/Political_activist "Political activist") known for using both psychedelic rock with [Highlife](/wiki/Highlife "Highlife") characteristics in his songs. His work often addressed themes relating to the socio-political climate of Nigeria in the 1970s.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Blo was a Nigerian [psychedelic funk](/wiki/Psychedelic_funk "Psychedelic funk") ensemble formed in [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos "Lagos") in 1972. The band consisted of Laolu "Akins" Akintobi (drums), Berkely "Ike" Jones (guitar) and Mike "Gbenga" Odumosu (bass). Their debut album, *Chapter One* (1973), drew inspiration from [Fela Kuti](/wiki/Fela_Kuti "Fela Kuti")'s Afrobeat and American [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"). The group later signed with Afrodisia and included more funk and [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") elements into their music. They disbanded in 1982.

[MonoMono](/wiki/MonoMono "MonoMono"), meaning "lightning" in [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_language "Yoruba language"), was a band formed in Lagos in 1971, led by [vocalist](/wiki/Vocalist "Vocalist") Joni Haastrup, using features of [Afropop](/wiki/Afropop "Afropop"), soul and [British rock](/wiki/British_rock_music "British rock music") and delivered in a [jam](/wiki/Jam_session "Jam session") format. Their debut album, *Give the Beggar a Chance* (1972), was a critical and commercial success, praised for its use of both traditional [Nigerian music](/wiki/Music_of_Nigeria "Music of Nigeria") with Western rock and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") influences.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Notable Afro rock musicians in Zambia (Zamrock)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Afro_rock&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notable Afro rock musicians in Zambia (Zamrock)")]

Main article: [Zamrock](/wiki/Zamrock "Zamrock")

In Zambia, Afro rock developed into a distinctive subgenre known as **[Zamrock](/wiki/Zamrock "Zamrock")**, or Zambian Rock, created in the late 1960s and early 1970s, which combined traditional Zambian melodies and Western rock music, including [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"), [garage rock](/wiki/Garage_rock "Garage rock"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"). Zamrock's growth to international fame was precipitated by the introduction of a [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") policy implemented by Zambia's first president, [Kenneth Kaunda](/wiki/Kenneth_Kaunda "Kenneth Kaunda"). In June 1975, Kaunda mandated that 95% of the music played on Zambian radio must be of local origin, which increased the production and promotion of the country's music industry.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/The_W.I.T.C.H_%282021%29.jpg/250px-The_W.I.T.C.H_%282021%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_W.I.T.C.H_(2021).jpg)

The Witch in 2021

Formed in the early 1970s in [Kitwe](/wiki/Kitwe "Kitwe"), [Witch](/wiki/Witch_(Zamrock_band) "Witch (Zamrock band)") (a backronym for "We Intend to Cause Havoc") became one of the most iconic Zamrock bands, known for their albums such as *Introduction* (1973) and *In the Past* (1974). After disbanding in the 1980s, the group saw a resurgence in popularity in the 2010s, with reissues of their music and new releases like *Zango* (2023) and *Sogolo* (2025).[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[Amanaz](/wiki/Amanaz "Amanaz") (an acronym of "Ask Me About Nice Artists in Zambia") was a five-piece band, led by Isaac Mpofu, from [Kitwe](/wiki/Kitwe "Kitwe") that released their only album, *Africa*, in 1975. The album is well-known for its music inspired by heavy rock, folk-pop, and funk and distinctive for its blues-like grooves and thick layers of [fuzz](/wiki/Fuzz_pedal "Fuzz pedal") guitar. Despite its initial limited commercial success, *Africa* has since gained recognition as a classic of the Zamrock genre.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Led by [Paul Ngozi](/wiki/Paul_Ngozi "Paul Ngozi"), The Ngozi Family was known for their energetic performances and their culturally significant [tracks](/wiki/Music_track "Music track") such as *Hi Babe* and *Day of Judgement* (1976).

## Notable Afro rock musicians in Ghana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Afro_rock&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notable Afro rock musicians in Ghana")]

Afro rock in [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") was created during the 1960s and 1970s, as a fusion genre of [Highlife](/wiki/Highlife "Highlife") music and elements of funk, rock, and [Afrobeat](/wiki/Afrobeat "Afrobeat").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Osibisa.jpg/250px-Osibisa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Osibisa.jpg)

Osibisa in 2008

[Osibisa](/wiki/Osibisa "Osibisa"), formed in [London](/wiki/London "London") in 1969 by Ghanaian musicians [Teddy Osei](/wiki/Teddy_Osei "Teddy Osei"), [Sol Amarfio](/wiki/Sol_Amarfio "Sol Amarfio"), and Mac Tontoh, alongside other Caribbean and Nigerian members, is one of the most well-known Afro rock bands.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The band's self-titled debut album, released in 1971, includes tracks such as *Music for Gong Gong* and *Sunshine Day*.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[Ebo Taylor](/wiki/Ebo_Taylor "Ebo Taylor"), a Ghanaian guitarist, composer, and bandleader active since the late 1950s, is an important figure in [Ghanaian music](/wiki/Ghanaian_music "Ghanaian music") during the 1960s and 1970s. He collaborated with several bands and worked as a producer and arranger for Essiebons Records.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The Psychedelic Aliens, also known as The Magic Aliens, were an experimental Ghanaian Afro rock group. Led by guitarist and vocalist Ade "Witch" Rocha, the band was known for hits such as *We're Laughing* and *Extraordinary Woman*.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Contemporary Afro rock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Afro_rock&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Contemporary Afro rock")]

In recent years, Afro Rock has seen a decline in popularity. However, the genre is still adopted by various artists, including laureates of the "Best Artist, Duo or Group" prize in the African Rock Category of the annual [All Africa Music Awards](/wiki/All_Africa_Music_Awards "All Africa Music Awards") (AFRIMA) such as the [Zimbabwean](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") Dear Zim (2014), the [Angolan](/wiki/Angola "Angola") M'vula (2015, 2016), the [Kenyan](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") Gilad (2017), the [Egyptian](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") Maryam Saleh (2018), the Kenyan Rash ([2021](/wiki/All_Africa_Music_Awards_2021 "All Africa Music Awards 2021")) and the Nigerian Clayrocksu ([2022](/wiki/All_Africa_Music_Awards_2022 "All Africa Music Awards 2022")). The 2022 laureate, [Clayrocksu](/wiki/Clayrocksu "Clayrocksu"), has gained international acclaim with features in the New York Times,[[44]](#cite_note-44) BBC News[[45]](#cite_note-45) and Reuters[[46]](#cite_note-46) for her innovative Afro Rock music, which references [Pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") folk and Igbo cultural elements and uses a punk rock style. The Canadian magazine *[Toronto Star](/wiki/Toronto_Star "Toronto Star")* defines her as "magnetic" for "her ability to fuse [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") with Afropop, the predominant contemporary sound in [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos "Lagos"), Nigeria".[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Another prominent contemporary [West African](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") rock band is Dark Suburb from [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), which takes inspiration from [Alkebulanian](/wiki/Alkebulan "Alkebulan") masquerade culture and skeletal imagery. Another distinguished African rock band is [Tinariwen](/wiki/Tinariwen "Tinariwen"), a collective of [Tuareg](/wiki/Tuareg_people "Tuareg people") musicians from the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") region of northern [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), known for their [desert blues](/wiki/Desert_blues "Desert blues") music, which combines traditional [Tuareg](/wiki/Tuareg_music "Tuareg music") and African music with Western [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"). They have released nine albums since their formation and have toured internationally. The group has been nominated for the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") three times, and their 2012 album *[Tassili](/wiki/Tassili_(album) "Tassili (album)")* won the award for [Best World Music Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Global_Music_Album "Grammy Award for Best Global Music Album") in 2012.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The group has been described by American media organization [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") as "music's true rebels"[[49]](#cite_note-49) and "rock 'n' roll rebels whose rebellion, for once, wasn't just metaphorical" by American online magazine *[Slate](/wiki/Slate_(magazine) "Slate (magazine)")*,[[50]](#cite_note-50) and their music has been claimed by [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") "a grassroots voice of rebellion".[[51]](#cite_note-:5-51)
